results.title: Chinese carrier Cosco shows interest in Santos superterminal auction
results.snippet: The tender, called Tecon Santos 10, is considered the largest port lease in Brazil's history. The new terminal is expected to increase container ...
results.summary:
# Chinese carrier Cosco shows interest in Santos superterminal auction
...
Cosco, the Chinese shipping giant and the world’s fourth-largest carrier, has signaled interest in bidding for the new container superterminal at the Port of Santos (SP).
The move came during a recent virtual meeting with Brazilian authorities but was accompanied by concerns: the company fears being barred from participating due to Antaq (National Waterway Transport Agency) regulations.
The agency’s restrictions prohibit companies with shareholdings in existing terminals from competing in the first phase.
Cosco holds a residual stake of less than 5% in a minority fund of a Santos terminal, which, in theory, would already exclude it from the bidding process.
**Historic auction**
...
At present, only the Philippine company ICTSI and Brazil’s JBS Terminais appear as firm contenders.
Other global operators such as Hapag-Lloyd and Hutchison Ports showed initial interest but withdrew.
Meanwhile, MSC and Maersk, the industry leaders, are barred from entering the auction since they already control the BTP terminal operating in Santos.
**International pressure**
...
The Court’s technical unit has already recommended relaxing the rules, allowing incumbent companies to participate as long as they divest from their current stakes in Santos.

results.title: Does booming air cargo corridor between China and Latin America ...
results.snippet: Air cargo spot rates from China to Latin America have more than doubled from May 2019 to May 2024. For the return leg, the spot rates from Latin America to ...
results.summary:
Air cargo spot rates from China to Latin America have more than doubled from May 2019 to May 2024.
For the return leg, the spot rates from Latin America to China have seen a low-double-digit increase compared to May 2019 levels.
The fronthaul rate growth is especially notable for Latin America’s largest economy — Brazil.
The average spot rate from China to Brazil reached USD 6.46 per kg in May – this is USD 1.60 per kg more expensive than the spot rate from China to the US.
This is in stark contrast with May 2019, when the average spot rate from China to Brazil was just above 40 cents more expensive.
Taking a month-on-month view, China to Brazil spot rates grew by 5% in May compared to April, again, outperforming the growth of China to the US spot rates, which showed a 2% decline.

results.title: Cosco launches new China – South America East Coast service
results.snippet: Cosco Shipping will deploy 12 vessels with a capacity of 14,000 teu each on the service, with weekly sailings. China has been Brazil's largest ...
results.summary:
Cosco Shipping has a new container service connecting Tianjin port in China to East Coast of South America.
May 13, 2024
A new service from the northern Chinese port of Tianjin to the East Coast of South America (ECSA) began operating last week to facilitate trade between China and countries in that region.
It will reduce sailing from 54 to 40 days and increase reefer shipping capacity.
The shipping route connecting China and South America - the longest across the Pacific Ocean - will be operated by Cosco Shipping, connecting the port of Tianjin with several ports in South America, including Rio de Janeiro, Santos, Itapoá and Navegantes in Brazil.
Cosco Shipping will deploy 12 vessels with a capacity of 14,000 teu each on the service, with weekly sailings.
China has been Brazil's largest trading partner for 15 consecutive years, and Brazil is also China's largest trading partner in Latin America and direct investment destination.

## Common Questions Answered
**Q: How long does shipping from China to Brazil actually take in 2025?** ** A: 5-10 days by air, 35-45 days by ocean (port-to-port), plus 5-15 days for customs clearance.**
**Q: What’s the cheapest way to ship from China to Brazil?** ** A: Ocean LCL for volumes under 15 CBM; FCL for larger shipments.
Avoid “too cheap” forwarders hidden fees are common.**

results.snippet: Our ocean freight services are fast, convenient, reliable, and competitively priced. CFC can move your cargo from China to key Brazilian ports at surprisingly ...
results.summary:
At CFC, we take pride in providing a full range of ocean shipping solutions to Brazil from China.
Whether you are shipping a Full Container Load (FCL) or Less than a Container Load (LCL), we have the right solutions for you.
- Port to port: This service covers the ocean shipping only.
- Door to port: This service includes domestic transportation in China, as well as ocean shipping.
- Port to door: This service covers ocean shipping plus domestic transportation in Brazil.
- Door to door: This service includes all transportation from the premises of the goods’ supplier/exporter to the premises of the recipient/importer.
As a provider of complete container logistics solutions, we make shipping easy for you by integrating ocean freight and road freight services into and across Brazil, so you don’t need to deal with multiple freight forwarders and carriers.
...
Our ocean freight services are fast, convenient, reliable, and competitively priced.
CFC can move your cargo from China to key Brazilian ports at surprisingly low shipping rates.
But your destination doesn’t need to be close to one of these ports.
Our door-to-door shipping container services combine ocean freight and road freight for a seamless international logistics experience.
...
LCL and FCL ocean freight take a lot longer to ship between China and Brazil, so it will not be ideal for urgent or unplanned shipments.
Sea freight transit times are typically between 30 and 40 days, depending on the port-to-port route used and the sea carrier.

results.title: Maersk sues over $1 billion Brazil port auction, MSC Group's TIL ...
results.snippet: Huge new Santos container terminal stirs dispute; Port auction rules favor newcomers over Maersk, MSC; Process may open door to Asian rivals ...
results.summary:
O PA, June25 () - International shipping companies are turning to Brazil's judiciary to challenge competition regulations that prevent them from engaging in the initial bidding phase for a significant new container terminal at Latin America’s largest port, set to occur later this year.
On Monday, the Danish shipping company Maersk (MAERSKb.CO) lodged a legal complaint in Sao Paulo against Brazil's maritime transport authority, known as Antaq, along with its general director, as indicated in a document reviewed by Reuters.
The lawsuit seeks "procedural adjustments to guarantee an equitable process" for the allocation of Tecon10 terminal the Port Santos.
…
The auction regulations would exclude Maersk, MSC, and other operators of current container terminals at Santos from the first bidding round for the construction and operation of the new megaterminal, which is anticipated to necessitate an investment of5.
billion re ($10 billion).
This exclusion could potentially benefit Asian competitors or a domestic entity like JBS Terminais, the newly established port operation division of Brazilian meat producer JBS (JBS.N), which acquired a container terminal in Itajaí, southern Brazil, last year.
JBS opted not to comment on the situation.
...
In the event that no valid bids are submitted during the initial phase of the auction, Antaq indicated that operators of existing container terminals at Santos would be permitted to participate in later rounds, contingent upon divesting their other interests within the port complex.
…
...
Eduardoon, director of Brazilian Coffeeers Council, stated that the organization is championing for "wide and unrestricted participation."
